verses.text: Then Elisha said, Hear ye the word of the LORD; Thus saith the LORD, To morrow about this time shall a measure of fine flour be sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel, in the gate of Samaria. Then a lord on whose hand the king leaned answered the man of God, and said, Behold, if the LORD would make windows in heaven, might this thing be? And he said, Behold, thou shalt see it with thine eyes, but shalt not eat thereof. Guidance: The reversal prophecy and the scoffing captain. Chapter 7 opens with Elisha's response to the king's blasphemy at the end of chapter 6. The king had said: 'what should I wait for the LORD any longer?' Elisha's reply now defines exactly what the king should wait for — and it will come within 24 hours. 'Thus saith the LORD, To morrow about this time shall a measure of fine flour be sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel, in the gate of Samaria.' The Hebrew se'ah-solet be-shekel ve-sa'tayim se'orim be-shekel — 'a seah of fine flour for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel.' A se'ah is approximately 7.3 liters / 1.9 gallons. At the previous chapter's famine prices (donkey's head 80 shekels), grain was effectively unavailable; the prophecy is that within 24 hours, fine flour will be selling at 1 shekel per 7 liters — ordinary peacetime prices. The reversal is total. A captain — 'a lord on whose hand the king leaned' (Hebrew shalish asher la-melekh nish'an al-yado) — is Jehoram's senior officer; the king is leaning on his hand, the standard royal-and-trusted-aide posture (the same role Naaman played for Ben-hadad in 5:18). This captain scoffs at the prophecy: 'Behold, if the LORD would make windows in heaven, might this thing be?' Hebrew hineh YHWH oseh arubot ba-shamayim ha-yihyeh ha-davar ha-zeh — 'behold the LORD making windows in the heavens — could this thing be?' The 'windows in heaven' image alludes to Gen 7:11 — the Flood — where the same Hebrew phrase describes the opening of the heavenly sluice-gates. The captain's mockery: even if the LORD opened the heavens like at the Flood, the situation could not turn around overnight. The starvation is too deep; the market collapse is too complete. Elisha's verdict on the captain: 'Behold, thou shalt see it with thine eyes, but shalt not eat thereof.' Hebrew hin'kha ro'eh be-einekha u-mi-sham lo tokhel — 'behold you will see with your eyes and from there you will not eat.' He will see the prophecy fulfilled but will not personally taste the food. The chapter is setting up two parallel verifications. (1) The market prices will fall exactly as Elisha specified. (2) The captain will witness the fulfillment but not partake. The dual prophecy will be exactly fulfilled in v17-20 — the captain will be trampled at the gate as the populace rushes out to plunder, seeing the food but not eating it. The chapter is teaching us that scoffing at the LORD's word has personal consequences. The prophet has now spoken twice: blessing for the city, judgment for the scoffer. Both will land within a day. The chapter is also showing us the LORD's restoration coming through the most unexpected channels. The next paragraph reveals that the agents of deliverance will not be the king's army or any human stratagem — they will be four lepers at the gate.

And there were four leprous men at the entering in of the gate: and they said one to another, Why sit we here until we die? If we say, We will enter into the city, then the famine is in the city, and we shall die there: and if we sit still here, we also die. Now therefore come, and let us fall unto the host of the Syrians: if they save us alive, we shall live; and if they kill us, we shall but die. Guidance: The four lepers' deliberation. The narrator shifts to four figures the city has otherwise forgotten. Four leprous men sit 'at the entering in of the gate.' Under Mosaic law (Lev 13:46), lepers were excluded from the camp; they lived 'without the camp' — outside the walls. In a besieged city the lepers had been driven out of normal society but trapped at the perimeter, unable to flee further because of the Aramean siege. They are at the gate but not in the city; they are within range of the Arameans but not yet seized. Their conversation is brutally rational. 'Why sit we here until we die?' Their three options: (1) enter the city — die of famine inside; (2) stay where they are — die of famine outside; (3) defect to the Aramean camp — either be killed there, or be allowed to live and be fed. The third option is the only one with any positive probability. They calculate cold-bloodedly: 'If they kill us, we shall but die' — death there is no worse than death anywhere else. 'If they save us alive, we shall live' — the Arameans might take them as slaves or feed them out of contempt. Worst-case outcome of all three options is identical; only option three contains a non-zero probability of survival. The chapter is showing us the calculus of utter desperation. People with nothing to lose can act on options that prosperous people would never consider. The lepers' marginalization has paradoxically positioned them perfectly for the discovery they are about to make: only people willing to walk into the enemy camp will find that the enemy camp is empty. The chapter is teaching us a striking biblical pattern: the LORD often uses the marginalized as His instruments of deliverance for the center. The four lepers — outcasts, useless to Samaria's defense, theologically unclean — will be the first to discover the LORD's overnight provision. They have nothing to lose; therefore they act; therefore they discover. The chapter is also setting up the comparison with the king. The king, surrounded by every resource, is paralyzed by despair. The four lepers, with nothing, act. The contrast between the throne and the gate is sharp.

And they rose up in the twilight, to go unto the camp of the Syrians: and when they were come to the uttermost part of the camp of Syria, behold, there was no man there. For the Lord had made the host of the Syrians to hear a noise of chariots, and a noise of horses, even the noise of a great host: and they said one to another, Lo, the king of Israel hath hired against us the kings of the Hittites, and the kings of the Egyptians, to come upon us. Wherefore they arose and fled in the twilight, and left their tents, and their horses, and their asses, even the camp as it was, and fled for their life. Guidance: The empty camp and the divine noise. The lepers set out 'in the twilight' (Hebrew ba-neshef — at dusk; the same term for both evening and dawn twilight, ambiguous here but likely evening). They reach the outer edge of the Aramean encampment. They expect to be challenged by sentries; instead, 'behold, there was no man there.' The Aramean army is gone. The narrator now provides the back-story the lepers do not yet know. 'The Lord had made the host of the Syrians to hear a noise of chariots, and a noise of horses, even the noise of a great host.' Hebrew va-adonai hishmia et-machaneh aram qol rekhev qol sus qol chayil gadol — 'the LORD caused the camp of Aram to hear the sound of chariots, the sound of horses, the sound of a great army.' An auditory hallucination/revelation — the Arameans heard the unmistakable sound of an approaching cavalry. Whether the LORD created the sound supernaturally or simply heightened their perception of distant ordinary sounds, the effect was overwhelming conviction that a massive relief force was approaching. Their interpretation: 'The king of Israel hath hired against us the kings of the Hittites, and the kings of the Egyptians, to come upon us.' The two major mercenary powers an Israelite king might have hired — the Hittites (still a significant fragmented power in southeast Anatolia) and the Egyptians (south, perpetually rivalrous with whatever Levantine state was rising) — were both plausible threats. Caught between Samaria's wall (with whatever army might sortie) and the supposed mercenary relief column approaching from outside, the Aramean position would have become indefensible. The result: panic-flight. 'They arose and fled in the twilight, and left their tents, and their horses, and their asses, even the camp as it was, and fled for their life.' Hebrew vayyanusu el-nafsham — 'they fled for their lives.' Total abandonment of everything: tents, draft animals, supplies, weapons. They ran. The chapter is showing us a victory achieved with no Israelite combat. The same LORD who fielded the fire-cavalry around Dothan (6:17) now creates a phantom cavalry on the Aramean side. The Arameans defeated themselves. The LORD's army did not need to engage; the rumor of an army was sufficient. The chapter is teaching us a profound principle. The LORD's victories often require no physical conflict at all. The Aramean siege — which had been starving Samaria for months, producing cannibalism, market collapse — was broken in a single night by sounds the Arameans heard and the Israelites did not. The deliverance arrived before the people knew it was coming.

And when these lepers came to the uttermost part of the camp, they went into one tent, and did eat and drink, and carried thence silver, and gold, and raiment, and went and hid it; and came again, and entered into another tent, and carried thence also, and went and hid it. Guidance: The lepers' first response: feast and loot. The four lepers, finding the camp empty, do what starving men would do. They enter the first tent — they eat (the abandoned Aramean meal still ready), they drink, they take silver, gold, and clothing, and they go off and hide it. They return; they enter a second tent; same procedure — eat, drink, take, hide. The chapter pauses to record their initial response without judgment. They are starving men in an abandoned enemy camp full of provisions. They eat first. They loot second. They hide third. They are not yet thinking about the city; they are not yet thinking about the strategic implications; they are not yet thinking about anyone except themselves. The chapter is teaching us about the natural sequence of relief. The first response of the rescued is to consume the resources of rescue. Hunger satisfies itself before it thinks about others. The lepers' acquisitive response is not yet morally wrong — they have not yet recognized that they are sitting on news that should be shared. They are still in survival mode. The chapter is also setting up the moment of conscience that will follow. In the next verse, the lepers will catch themselves and realize that what they are doing is incomplete. The deliverance is not theirs to hoard; the news must be shared. The chapter is showing us the trajectory of moral awakening: first satisfy immediate need, then begin to perceive larger obligation. The lepers cannot share until they themselves have eaten. The chapter does not condemn the eating; it shows the eating as the necessary first stage before the moral perception can develop.

Then they said one to another, We do not well: this day is a day of good tidings, and we hold our peace: if we tarry till the morning light, some mischief will come upon us: now therefore come, that we may go and tell the king's household. So they came, and called unto the porter of the city: and he told it to them within, saying, We came to the camp of the Syrians, and, behold, there was no man there, neither voice of man, but horses tied, and asses tied, and the tents as they were. Guidance: Conscience and the gospel-impulse. After two tents' worth of feasting and looting, the lepers stop. Their conscience awakens. 'We do not well.' Hebrew lo-khen anachnu osim — 'not so are we doing.' Their conduct is wrong. The diagnostic phrase that follows is one of the most theologically loaded in the OT narrative literature: 'This day is a day of good tidings.' Hebrew ha-yom ha-zeh yom besorah hu — 'this day is a day of besorah.' The noun besorah is the Hebrew root that gives Greek euangelion ('gospel') and English 'gospel.' This day is a 'gospel day' — a day of good news. The lepers recognize that they are sitting on news the city needs. To hold the news for themselves is the wrong response to this kind of day. 'If we tarry till the morning light, some mischief will come upon us.' If they continue to delay, judgment will catch up with them — either external (the king finding out they sat on the news), or moral (the burden of their selfishness). 'Now therefore come, that we may go and tell the king's household.' They resolve to bring the news to the city. They walk to the city gate. 'They called unto the porter of the city.' The gatekeeper. They deliver their report: 'We came to the camp of the Syrians, and, behold, there was no man there, neither voice of man, but horses tied, and asses tied, and the tents as they were.' Brief, factual, complete: no army, no sound, animals still tethered, tents undisturbed — the camp abandoned with everything in place. The chapter is teaching us multiple foundational principles. (1) The good news of deliverance must be shared. When the LORD provides relief, those who first discover it have an obligation to tell others. To hoard the news is wrong. (2) The lepers' phrase — 'a day of good tidings' — is the OT root that the NT will inherit. The pattern of authentic 'gospel' announcement is established: people who have personally tasted the LORD's provision tell others where to find it. The Christian evangelistic impulse is rooted in this exact scene: starving outcasts who have found bread must not eat alone. (3) Self-correction by conscience is a mark of authentic faith. The lepers were not commanded by an outsider to share; they recognized for themselves that hoarding was wrong. The chapter is showing us that even the marginalized — when their souls are alive — develop the impulse to share what they have found.

And he called the porters; and they told it to the king's house within. Guidance: The relay system. The gate-porter relays the report to the other porters; collectively they pass the news inward through the gate-guard system to 'the king's house within' (Hebrew beit ha-melekh penimah). Israelite gate-systems typically had multiple porters or watchmen at the main gate; messages of military importance traveled inward through this chain to the palace. The chapter is showing us the LORD's news working its way from the margin to the center. The four outcasts at the gate cannot themselves walk into the palace; their report must travel through the watchmen, then through the inner palace staff, until it reaches the king. The chapter is teaching us how good news propagates. It does not arrive at the center in one step; it works through layers of relay. The lepers had to find the porter; the porter had to find the other porters; they together had to relay to the palace; the palace staff had to inform the king. Each step preserves and transmits the message. The chapter is also preserving the implicit detail that the news traveled fast — fast enough that by morning the king was already responding. The infrastructure of communication, even in this collapsing city, functioned.

And the king arose in the night, and said unto his servants, I will now shew you what the Syrians have done to us. They know that we be hungry; therefore are they gone out of the camp to hide themselves in the field, saying, When they come out of the city, we shall catch them alive, and get into the city. And one of his servants answered and said, Let some take, I pray thee, five of the horses that remain, which are left in the city, (behold, they are as all the multitude of Israel that are left in it: behold, I say, they are even as all the multitude of the Israelites that are consumed:) and let us send and see. Guidance: The king suspects a trap. The king receives the report and rises in the night. His response is paranoid: 'I will now shew you what the Syrians have done to us. They know that we be hungry; therefore are they gone out of the camp to hide themselves in the field, saying, When they come out of the city, we shall catch them alive, and get into the city.' Jehoram is convinced the empty-camp report is an Aramean ruse. He reasons: starvation has weakened us; the Arameans know it; they have pretended to retreat to lure us out; when we sortie to plunder, they will ambush us; with our gates open, they will rush in and seize the city. The chapter is showing us the king's pessimism. Even when good news arrives, his framework rejects it as too good to be true. His theological poverty — already revealed in the previous chapter — extends to his interpretive instincts. He cannot recognize the LORD's deliverance when it lands at his gate. One of his servants proposes a test: 'Let some take, I pray thee, five of the horses that remain, which are left in the city... and let us send and see.' Five surviving horses — out of what was presumably a much larger royal stable, the famine has reduced the count to these — should be sent to scout. The phrase 'behold, they are as all the multitude of Israel that are left in it' is the servant's grim assessment: these five horses are about as many as the Israelites left in the city. The siege has reduced both human and equine populations to a remnant. The chapter is teaching us about the wisdom of measured verification. The servant does not say 'yes' to either alternative (the news is real / the news is a trap); he proposes empirical investigation. Five horses. Five scouts. Find out. The chapter is also exposing the king's mental state. His first instinct is conspiracy theory. The servant's first instinct is investigation. The servant is wiser.

And the king arose in the night, and said unto his servants, I will now shew you what the Syrians have done to us. They know that we be hungry; therefore are they gone out of the camp to hide themselves in the field, saying, When they come out of the city, we shall catch them alive, and get into the city. And one of his servants answered and said, Let some take, I pray thee, five of the horses that remain, which are left in the city, (behold, they are as all the multitude of Israel that are left in it: behold, I say, they are even as all the multitude of the Israelites that are consumed:) and let us send and see. Guidance: The king suspects a trap. The king receives the report and rises in the night. His response is paranoid: 'I will now shew you what the Syrians have done to us. They know that we be hungry; therefore are they gone out of the camp to hide themselves in the field, saying, When they come out of the city, we shall catch them alive, and get into the city.' Jehoram is convinced the empty-camp report is an Aramean ruse. He reasons: starvation has weakened us; the Arameans know it; they have pretended to retreat to lure us out; when we sortie to plunder, they will ambush us; with our gates open, they will rush in and seize the city. The chapter is showing us the king's pessimism. Even when good news arrives, his framework rejects it as too good to be true. His theological poverty — already revealed in the previous chapter — extends to his interpretive instincts. He cannot recognize the LORD's deliverance when it lands at his gate. One of his servants proposes a test: 'Let some take, I pray thee, five of the horses that remain, which are left in the city... and let us send and see.' Five surviving horses — out of what was presumably a much larger royal stable, the famine has reduced the count to these — should be sent to scout. The phrase 'behold, they are as all the multitude of Israel that are left in it' is the servant's grim assessment: these five horses are about as many as the Israelites left in the city. The siege has reduced both human and equine populations to a remnant. The chapter is teaching us about the wisdom of measured verification. The servant does not say 'yes' to either alternative (the news is real / the news is a trap); he proposes empirical investigation. Five horses. Five scouts. Find out. The chapter is also exposing the king's mental state. His first instinct is conspiracy theory. The servant's first instinct is investigation. The servant is wiser.

They took therefore two chariot horses; and the king sent after the host of the Syrians, saying, Go and see. And they went after them unto Jordan: and, lo, all the way was full of garments and vessels, which the Syrians had cast away in their haste. And the messengers returned, and told the king. Guidance: The scouts and the trail. Out of the five remaining horses, the king's officers harness two to a chariot — perhaps the most pragmatic scout-team possible (two horses pulling a chariot can move faster and farther than horses ridden separately by tired men). The chariot is dispatched: 'Go and see.' The scouts follow the route an Aramean flight would have taken. Aram lies northeast of Samaria; the natural escape route led east-northeast across the central hills, then down to the Jordan, then across into Aramean territory. The chariot follows this route. The discovery: 'All the way was full of garments and vessels, which the Syrians had cast away in their haste.' The flight had been so panicked that the Arameans had abandoned not just their camp but their personal gear during the retreat. Cloaks, weapons, drinking vessels, supplies — the artifacts of a rout littered the road for 30 miles. The Arameans had run not just from their camp but to the Jordan, shedding equipment to lighten themselves for speed. The chapter is teaching us how comprehensive the LORD's overnight victory was. This was not just a tactical withdrawal; this was a panicked rout. The auditory hallucination had so terrified the Arameans that they ran 30 miles, abandoning equipment along the way. The scouts could not catch up to them; the road told the story. The chapter is also providing the empirical confirmation that turns the king's paranoia into impossibility. If this had been an ambush ruse, the road would not have been littered with abandoned gear. The trail of equipment proves the flight is real. The scouts return; they report; the verification is complete.

And the people went out, and spoiled the tents of the Syrians. So a measure of fine flour was sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel, according to the word of the LORD. Guidance: The market prices fulfilled. The verification triggers the response. 'The people went out, and spoiled the tents of the Syrians.' The starving population pours through the city gates and plunders the abandoned camp. They bring back food, livestock, weapons, treasure. Within hours, the city's economic situation flips completely. The narrator's punchline: 'So a measure of fine flour was sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel, according to the word of the LORD.' Hebrew ki-devar YHWH — 'according to the word of the LORD.' Elisha's prophecy from the previous day — the prophecy that the captain had mocked as impossible — has been fulfilled exactly. The Hebrew prices match the Hebrew of v1 word-for-word. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's prophetic words are not approximate; they are precise. The market prices Elisha named yesterday are the market prices that hold today. Specific commodity (fine flour, barley); specific quantities (one seah, two seahs); specific cost (one shekel each); specific location (the gate of Samaria); specific timing ('tomorrow about this time'). All of it confirmed. The chapter is showing us prophecy verified by economic data. The siege had distorted prices to crisis levels; the LORD's overnight intervention has restored normal prices. The covenant curse has been lifted; the covenant blessing has returned. The chapter is also setting up the second half of the prophecy — the captain's witnessing-without-eating — which the next paragraph will fulfill.

And the king appointed the lord on whose hand he leaned to have the charge of the gate: and the people trode upon him in the gate, and he died, as the man of God had said, who spake when the king came down to him. And it came to pass as the man of God had spoken to the king, saying, Two measures of barley for a shekel, and a measure of fine flour for a shekel, shall be to morrow about this time in the gate of Samaria: And that lord answered the man of God, and said, Now, behold, if the LORD should make windows in heaven, might such a thing be? And he said, Behold, thou shalt see it with thine eyes, but shalt not eat thereof. And so it fell out unto him: for the people trode upon him in the gate, and he died. Guidance: The captain trampled and the dual prophecy fulfilled. The king assigns the scoffing captain — the senior officer who had mocked Elisha's prophecy — to manage the city gate during the people's rush to the spoil. The gate is the bottleneck; thousands of starving citizens are pouring out and back; the assignment requires crowd-control. The captain is doing his duty. But the famished crowd, in their rush to reach the food, tramples him to death at the gate. Hebrew vayyirmesuhu ha-am ba-sha'ar vayyamot — 'the people trampled him in the gate, and he died.' The narrator deliberately rehearses the entire dual prophecy in v18-19 — repeating Elisha's two-part prediction verbatim — and then concludes in v20: 'And so it fell out unto him: for the people trode upon him in the gate, and he died.' Both halves of the prophecy fulfilled. (1) He saw the abundance with his eyes — the crowd was rushing through the gate carrying back the spoil; the captain was at the heart of the abundance; he saw it. (2) He did not eat thereof — he was trampled to death before he could partake. The chapter is teaching us that scoffing at the LORD's word is not a costless intellectual posture. The captain had used 'windows in heaven' to mock divine provision; he did not survive the day the provision arrived. The same gate through which life flowed for thousands was the gate where he died. The chapter is also closing the long arc that began in 6:24 with Ben-hadad's siege. The siege has been broken; the famine has been reversed; the cannibalism is past; the market prices are restored; the prophet's word has been vindicated in every detail; and the scoffer who mocked the prophet has been trampled by the very crowd whose lives were saved. The chapter is showing us a comprehensive vindication of prophetic ministry. Every word Elisha spoke under the LORD's hand has come true: the relief, the timing, the prices, the captain's witness, the captain's death. The chapter is teaching us a final principle. The LORD's prophets do not need to defend their words; the LORD vindicates their words by the events that follow. Elisha did not stage a counter-argument with the captain in 7:2; he simply named the captain's fate. The chapter has now traced the journey from oracle (v2) through detailed fulfillment (v17-20). The reader closes the chapter with renewed confidence that the LORD's word is reliable. The chapter is also previewing 2 Kings as a whole: prophecies fulfilled, kings vindicated or judged, the LORD's faithfulness demonstrated through historical specifics.
